did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper 'A CLIDIPA multi-centre validation study on deep learning-based classification of early-stage mycosis fungoides and benign inflammatory dermatoses' explicitly states that deep-learning (DL) models were trained using 2,315 H&E-stained whole-slide images to classify early-stage mycosis fungoides (MF) versus benign inflammatory dermatoses (BIDs). It also mentions external validation using WSIs from the CLIDIPA Registry.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is a conference abstract presenting original experimental results from a multi-centre validation study. It details the training of deep-learning models on 2,315 whole-slide images and their external validation using data from the CLIDIPA Registry to classify early-stage mycosis fungoides versus benign inflammatory dermatoses. The abstract reports specific methods, dataset sizes, and validation procedures, which constitutes original research rather than a review of prior literature.

title: A CLIDIPA multi-centre validation study on deep learning-based classification of early-stage mycosis fungoides and benign inflammatory dermatoses
brief_summary: This study addresses the diagnostic challenge of distinguishing early-stage mycosis fungoides from benign inflammatory dermatoses using deep learning. Researchers trained deep-learning models on 2,315 H&E-stained whole-slide images from Leiden University Medical Centre. The models were externally validated using 615 whole-slide images from the CLIDIPA Registry across five European centers. The primary goal was to benchmark the AI-assisted classification performance against expert pathologists. The paper presents the methodology and dataset details for this multi-centre validation study.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the deep learning classification task. Diagnoses established by expert clinicopathological consensus. Included in the training set (633 WSIs from 317 patients) and the external validation set from the CLIDIPA Registry.

The comparator class for the deep learning classification task, used to distinguish from early-stage mycosis fungoides. Included in the training set (1682 WSIs from 1446 patients) and the external validation set from the CLIDIPA Registry.

all_task_types.target: Diagnosis of early-stage mycosis fungoides (MF) versus benign inflammatory dermatoses (BIDs)
all_task_types.details: The study trains deep-learning models to classify H&E-stained whole-slide images into two discrete diagnostic categories: early-stage mycosis fungoides and benign inflammatory dermatoses. The task is explicitly described as 'classification' in the title and abstract, with the goal of benchmarking AI-assisted classification performance against expert pathologists.

datasets_used.name: Leiden University Medical Centre (LUMC) Cohort
datasets_used.description: Internal training dataset consisting of H&E-stained whole-slide images (WSIs) from Leiden University Medical Centre, with diagnoses established by expert clinicopathological consensus.

datasets_used.name: CLIDIPA Registry (External Validation Cohort)
datasets_used.description: External validation dataset obtained from the Cutaneous Lymphoma International Digital Pathology (CLIDIPA) Registry, comprising WSIs from five European centers: Utrecht, Zurich, Würzburg, Turin, and Minden.

use.testing: Used for external validation of the deep-learning models.
use.other: A subset of 175 cases was independently reviewed by eleven (dermato-)pathologists using a 5-point scale to benchmark model performance.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess statistical significance of differences in skin toxicity risk between MF/SS and LMS, and to evaluate associations between overall response rate (ORR) and cutaneous toxicity, blood involvement, or large-cell transformation.
To estimate the uncertainty around the median time to systemic therapy (TTST).
To describe the distribution of continuous variables such as age, LDH, CD4+CD26-, CD4+CD7-, white blood cells, and lymphocytes.
To describe categorical variables such as sex, MF subtype, stage, comorbidities, and incidence of cutaneous toxicity.

hypothesis_testing.test: Statistical significance testing (specifically p-values reported for associations and differences)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no association between the variables being compared (e.g., no difference in skin toxicity risk between MF/SS and LMS; no association between ORR and cutaneous toxicity/blood involvement/large-cell transformation; no association between progression and factors other than %CD4+CD26- and %CD4+CD7-; no occurrence of infusion reactions in patients with cutaneous toxicity).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in skin toxicity risk between MF/SS and LMS; there is an association between ORR and cutaneous toxicity/blood involvement/large-cell transformation; progression is associated with baseline %CD4+CD26- and %CD4+CD7-; there is an association between cutaneous toxicity and infusion reactions.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Significance was interpreted using p-values. Differences in skin toxicity risk between MF/SS and LMS were not statistically significant (p-values not explicitly given for the comparison but stated as 'not statistically significant'). Progressors showed significantly higher baseline %CD4+CD26- and %CD4+CD7- (p = 0.0003 for both). ORR was not associated with cutaneous toxicity (p = 0.34), blood involvement (p = 0.69), or large-cell transformation (p = 0.26). No patients with cutaneous toxicity experienced infusion reactions (p = 0.02).

training_details.dataset: 2,315 H&E-stained whole-slide images from Leiden University Medical Centre
training_details.classes: Early-stage mycosis fungoides (633 WSIs) and benign inflammatory dermatoses (1,682 WSIs)

conclusions: The study presents the methodology and dataset details for a multi-centre validation study on deep learning-based classification of early-stage mycosis fungoides (eMF) versus benign inflammatory dermatoses (BIDs), but does not report the final quantitative performance results (e.g., AUC, sensitivity, specificity) of the models in the provided text.

Deep learning models were trained on 2,315 H&E-stained whole-slide images from Leiden University Medical Centre (633 eMF and 1,682 BIDs) with diagnoses established by expert clinicopathological consensus.
External validation was conducted using 615 whole-slide images from the CLIDIPA Registry across five European centers (Utrecht, Zurich, Würzburg, Turin, and Minden).
Model performance was benchmarked against eleven (dermato-)pathologists who independently reviewed a subset of 175 cases using a 5-point scale reflecting diagnostic inclination and uncertainty.
The study highlights the diagnostic challenge of distinguishing eMF from BIDs due to clinical and histopathological overlap, underscoring the potential for AI-assisted classification.
important_remarks: The provided text is a conference abstract that outlines the methodology and dataset composition but does not report the final quantitative performance results (e.g., AUC, sensitivity, specificity) of the deep learning models.
The study lacks specific details on the deep learning architecture (e.g., backbone, layers) and training hyperparameters (e.g., optimizer, loss function).

The external validation dataset (CLIDIPA Registry) is geographically limited to five European centers (Utrecht, Zurich, Würzburg, Turin, Minden), which may limit generalizability to other populations.
The benchmarking against pathologists was performed on a small subset of 175 cases, which may not fully represent the diagnostic difficulty of the entire external validation cohort.

The paper does not provide statistical metrics (such as confidence intervals or p-values) for the deep learning model's performance, only for the pathologist benchmark setup and the separate toxicity study (Chunk 0-3) which appears to be a different abstract mixed in the context.
